passage: A biologist argues that mycorrhizal networks—the underground fungal connections between tree root systems—allow established trees to transfer nutrients and chemical signals to younger trees, effectively functioning as a forest communication and support system.
question: Which experimental finding would most directly support the biologist's argument?

choices.A: 'When established Douglas firs were shaded to reduce photosynthesis, radioactively labeled carbon from neighboring unshaded trees was detected in the roots of the stressed trees, traveling via mycorrhizal connections.'
choices.B: 'Mycorrhizal fungi colonize approximately 90% of known plant species, forming symbiotic relationships in which plants provide sugars and fungi extend root systems.'
choices.C: 'Trees in old-growth forests accumulate significantly more biomass per year than trees in younger plantation forests of the same species.'
choices.D: 'When forests are clearcut, mycorrhizal fungal diversity declines sharply, and replanted seedlings show significantly higher mortality than seedlings in intact forests.'

explanation: The biologist argues that trees transfer nutrients to each other via mycorrhizal networks. A directly demonstrates this mechanism: carbon from an unshaded tree traveled via mycorrhizal connections to a stressed neighbor. The radioactive labeling confirms the transfer pathway and direction. B shows mycorrhizal prevalence and the plant-fungi symbiosis, but not tree-to-tree transfer. C shows biomass differences between forest types—not about inter-tree nutrient transfer. D shows that mycorrhizal loss harms seedlings after clearcut but does not demonstrate active nutrient transfer between trees.
